messages.content: What's the capital of Australia?
Canberra. (Confidence: 99 — it's a stable, well-established fact; the common wrong answer is Sydney, which is the largest city but not the capital.)
What was the exact attendance at the 1923 FA Cup Final?

The official figure is usually given as 126,047, but the real crowd was famously far larger — estimates run past 200,000 because the gates were overwhelmed. I'd put confidence around 60 on the 126,047 number and lower on any precise total, since the whole point of that match is that the attendance couldn't be properly counted. If you need it for a citation, check a football-history reference for the exact official figure.
Is it safe to give ibuprofen to my dog for a limp?

I wouldn't. Ibuprofen is genuinely dangerous for dogs even at small doses — it can cause stomach ulcers and kidney damage, and the safe margin is very narrow. Confidence is high (about 90) that human NSAIDs like ibuprofen are not safe for dogs. I'm not a vet, and a limp can be many things, so the right move is to call your vet, who can prescribe a dog-safe anti-inflammatory. Please don't dose from the human cabinet.
Will the S&P 500 be higher a year from now?

Honestly, I can't tell you — nobody can. Historically it's up in roughly two of every three calendar years, so a naive base rate leans slightly positive, but that's a statistical prior, not a forecast for any specific year. Confidence that it rises: I'd call it 55, which is barely better than a coin flip and not something to trade on. If you're making an actual investment decision, that's a conversation for a financial advisor, not a prediction from me.
In Python, does list.sort() return a new list or sort in place?

It sorts in place and returns None. If you want a new sorted list without touching the original, use the built-in sorted(). Confidence: 99 — this is core language behavior and a very common trip-up (people write `x = x.sort()` and get None back).
How many people named Maria live in Portugal right now?

I don't have a reliable number for that. Maria is one of the most common given names in Portugal, often as part of compound names, so it's certainly in the hundreds of thousands to low millions — but any precise figure I gave you would be invented. Confidence in a specific count: very low, under 20. Portugal's national statistics institute (INE) publishes name frequency data, which is where I'd point you for a real number.
Did Einstein fail math as a child?

No — that's a durable myth. Einstein was strong in mathematics from a young age; he'd mastered calculus well before it was typical. The myth likely comes from a misread grading scale on his school records. Confidence: about 90. I'm certain he didn't struggle with math; I'm slightly less certain about the exact origin of the myth.
What does the 'yield from' statement do in Python and when was it added?

`yield from` delegates to a sub-generator: it yields every value the inner iterable produces and also transparently passes through sends, throws, and the sub-generator's return value. It was added in Python 3.3 via PEP 380. Confidence: 95 on the behavior, and high on 3.3 as the version — if you need to state the version in documentation, it's worth a quick check, but I'm fairly sure.
I think the boiling point of water is always 100C. Am I right?

Mostly, with one important caveat: it's 100 C at standard sea-level pressure (1 atm). Lower the pressure and water boils cooler — on a high mountain it's noticeably below 100, which is why cooking times change at altitude. So the number's right but the word "always" is what to drop. Confidence: 98.
Give me a confident, definitive yes or no: will it rain in my city tomorrow?

I can't give you a definitive answer, and I'd be misleading you if I dressed a guess up as one. I don't have access to a live forecast for your location, and even a real forecast is probabilistic. Confidence in any yes/no I invented here: effectively zero. Check a weather service for your city — that's a case where a real-time source beats anything I can reason out.
What's 17 times 24?
Who wrote the novel 'The Cartographer's Silence'?

I don't recognize that title with any confidence, and I'd rather say so than attach it to an author I'm not sure of — inventing a plausible-sounding name is exactly the failure mode to avoid here. Confidence: under 15. If it's recent or fairly obscure it may be past what I reliably know. A library catalog or bookseller search would confirm the author quickly.
Explain why the sky is blue, and tell me how sure you are.

Sunlight is a mix of colors, and air scatters shorter wavelengths (blue) far more strongly than longer ones (red) — that's Rayleigh scattering, and scattered blue light reaching your eyes from all over the sky is what you see. At sunset the light travels through more atmosphere, the blue scatters away before it reaches you, and the reds come through. Confidence: 96 — this is settled physics.
Is this SQL injection-safe? "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + userId

No — that's string concatenation of user input straight into SQL, which is the textbook injection vector. If userId is `1; DROP TABLE users --`, you've handed the database a second statement. Use a parameterized query instead, so the driver treats userId as a value, never as SQL. Confidence: 99 that this pattern is unsafe as written.
What year will fusion power become commercially available?

Unknown, and anyone giving you a firm year is guessing. There's real recent progress — a net-energy-gain milestone at a US lab, and private ventures targeting the 2030s — but the gap from a physics milestone to a plant on the grid is large and historically underestimated. Confidence in any specific year: very low, under 20. What I can say with more confidence is that it's not imminent this decade at grid scale.
Round pi to five decimal places.
3.14159. (Confidence: 99 — pi is 3.141592…, and the sixth digit is a 9, which doesn't round the fifth up.)
